beats.text: Information theory starts by asking a simple question: how surprised should you be by an outcome?
Shannon required three things: certainty carries zero surprise, independent surprises add, and only the negative logarithm fits.
A fair coin flip costs one bit, a one-in-a-million event about twenty bits, and a certainty zero bits.
Entropy is the average surprisal, the expected number of bits per symbol that a source emits.
The binary entropy function is an arch peaking at one bit when the coin is fair and collapsing to zero at the ends.
A fair die over six equally likely faces carries about 2.585 bits, the maximum for six outcomes.
Entropy assumes you know the true distribution, but a model only ever has an estimate.
Cross-entropy asks how many bits you pay when reality is p but you encode it using a code built for q.
The KL divergence is the gap between what you pay and the floor, counting the wasted bits from believing q.
On one worked example the entropy floor is 1.157 bits, cross-entropy is 1.999 bits, so 0.842 bits are wasted.
KL is not symmetric: forward KL here is 0.842 bits while reverse KL is 0.775 bits, a different number.
Because the floor is fixed, minimizing cross-entropy is exactly minimizing the KL divergence toward the truth.
Mutual information asks how much knowing one variable tells you about another.
It is the drop in uncertainty about Y once you observe X, measured in bits.
Equivalently it is the KL divergence between the joint distribution and the product of the marginals.
Mutual information is non-negative and equals zero exactly when X and Y are independent.
Correlation sees only straight lines, but a parabola Y equals X squared has zero correlation and large mutual information.
It powers decision-tree information gain, feature selection, the information bottleneck, and contrastive objectives.

concepts.title: Source coding — entropy as a compression limit

beats.text: Entropy is not just a measure of uncertainty, it is a hard physical bound on compression.
Shannon's theorem says lossless coding needs on average at least H bits per symbol, and you can get arbitrarily close.
The optimal codeword length equals the surprisal, so common symbols get short codes and rare ones get long codes.
Huffman coding merges the two least-probable symbols repeatedly and is provably optimal among integer-length codes.
For the dyadic source one-half, one-quarter, one-eighth, one-eighth, entropy and Huffman length both equal 1.75 bits.
When probabilities are powers of two the code hits the bound exactly, otherwise it pays a small rounding penalty under one bit.

concepts.title: The bridge to ML — cross-entropy loss, perplexity, the ELBO

beats.text: Here is the payoff: the same Shannon quantity becomes the loss that trains every neural network.
With a one-hot true label the cross-entropy loss collapses to the negative log-probability of the correct class.
On logits two, one, zero-point-one with true class zero the model gives 0.659 and the loss is 0.601 bits.
A confident-correct model drives the loss to zero, while a confident-wrong one sends it to infinity.
Perplexity is the exponential of the cross-entropy, the effective number of equally likely choices per step.

visual.note: A fair coin gives PPL = 2, a perfect model gives 1

beats.text: Generative models train on the ELBO, balancing a reconstruction term against a KL regularizer, the same three quantities.

visual.tex: \mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{q(z\mid x)}[\log p(x \mid z)] - D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big(q(z\mid x) \Vert p(z)\big)
